comments:
Summary:
The manuscript presents DAC-h3, a layered cognitive architecture for human-robot interaction implemented on the iCub robot. The system combines a biologically-inspired Distributed Adaptive Control (DAC) organization (somatic, reactive, adaptive, contextual layers) with a collection of state-of-the-art perceptual, motor and language modules (object recognition, synthetic sensory memory, language reservoir handlers, autobiographical memory, action rendering engine, etc.). Two intrinsic drives (knowledge acquisition and expression) produce proactive mixed-initiative behaviors that bootstrap multimodal grounding (visual, tactile, motor, linguistic) of predefined entities and support goal-directed plans and narrative generation. The implementation runs on real hardware and code is available.

Strengths:
- The paper presents a comprehensive, end-to-end system running on a real humanoid (iCub). The integration of many modules into a coherent architecture is substantial engineering and conceptual work.
- The DAC-based layered design is well motivated and aligns with biologically-inspired principles; the use of intrinsic drives to produce proactive behavior is persuasive and links to the symbol grounding motivation.
- The system supports a broad set of capabilities (lexicon acquisition, viewpoint reasoning, action recognition, simple planning, autobiographical memory and narrative) and demonstrates mixed-initiative interactions on a real robot. Open-source code is an important practical contribution.
- The paper is generally well organized and explains the roles of modules and the flow between layers clearly.

Weaknesses / Limitations:
- The contribution is primarily an integration and instantiation of existing modules rather than new algorithms; while this is valuable, the novelty is in the architecture and integration rather than methodological advances of individual components.
- Evaluation is demonstrated via scenario descriptions and videos, but lacks quantitative and statistical analyses. The authors acknowledge this; however, the paper would be strengthened by objective metrics (e.g., success rates for grounding, recognition accuracy, time to acquire labels, user satisfaction measures) and by ablation studies showing the effect of proactivity or different modules.
- Comparative evaluation is limited. There is discussion of related work, but no empirical comparison against alternative architectures/approaches or ablated variants (e.g., reactive-only vs. proactive). This weakens claims about advantages of the approach.
- The system presupposes an a priori symbolic ontology of entities (objects, agents, body parts). While the architecture grounds multimodal attributes and names, it does not tackle the problem of discovering entity categories or segmentation from raw streams; the authors note this. This reduces the scope with respect to the full Symbol Grounding Problem.
- Some components use engineered/planned behaviors (predefined action plans) rather than learned policies; moving towards learned planning/action selection would improve biological plausibility and autonomy.
- Details about robustness to real-world noise (speech recognition errors, cluttered scenes, multiple humans) and processing latencies are not extensively discussed.

Suggestions for improvement / future work:
- Provide quantitative experiments: success rates for name grounding, action recognition accuracy, reaction times, number of interactions needed to learn a label, and user study data for acceptability/predictability.
- Include ablation studies demonstrating the contribution of the proactive drives and the layered architecture (e.g., turn off knowledge-expression drive or remove contextual planning) to validate claims about proactivity and drive regulation.
- Investigate mechanisms for discovering entities/categories from raw sensor data (so the system does not require predefined entity types), and move from predefined plans to learned policies or rules as the authors mention in future work.
- Provide empirical comparisons (even if limited) to other HRI systems or baselines to strengthen claims of improvement.
- Report more details on robustness and limitations: failure modes, system latency, and behavior under noisy speech or occlusion.

Recommendation:
This paper is a solid systems and integration contribution to cognitive robotics and HRI. It will be of interest to researchers building embodied language grounding and mixed-initiative robotic systems. I recommend acceptance (strong accept) provided the venue accepts systems contributions; the manuscript would benefit from expanded quantitative evaluation in a revised version.
